text: The first step is to catch hold of the azagus vein and dissect it completely from all sides, clip it and cut it.
Once the azagus vein is clipped and cut, it is lifted up with the help of a suture, hanged on to the mochi's needle and retracted out of the
thoracic cavity.
The next step is to dissect close to the ozygous vein up to the prevascular fascia and exposing the whitening glistening white of the iota.
The prevascular fascia is further defined up towards cranial side all along the left side lung and the left pleura.
The prevascular fascia continues from the aorta to the descending or the arch of the aorta and up to the left side of the pleura.

This part of the dissection is very important where you can see that we are dissecting not the prevascular fascia but mistakenly gone into the previsceral fascia which also is an avascular fascia and can be mistaken for a prevascular fascia.

Now you can see that we are dissecting not the visceral fascia but the actual plane that is the prevascular fascia which is also a continuation of the aorta, the arch of the aorta and the left side pleura and all the fatty tissue is taken down from the left side pleura.
along into the esophagus.

The next step is to isolate the lymph nodal packet from the esophago-tracheal groove where the esophagus is lifted up, the trachea is down and the esophago-tracheal ligament is dissected as seen by the black arrow.
This is carefully done not to rupture or to injure the posterior layer of the trachea which is the membranous part of the trachea.
This is an avascular zone only containing small arteries, capillaries which supply the esophagus and the trachea.
The whole of the esophago-tracheal ligament is cut and the esophagus is lifted up.
The next step is to cut along the visceral fascia to isolate the left laryngeal lymph nodule packet from the medial side.
Previously, we had isolated the packet from the lateral side, that is the prevascular fascia.
And now we are isolating or releasing all the adhesions from the medial side, staying close to the trachea and lifting all the left recurrent laryngeal lymph nodal packet up along with the esophagus.
Within the left recurrent laryngeal lymph nodal packet, you can see the left recurrent laryngeal nerve coming out.
Now the dissection happens both ventrally as well as dorsal to the left recurrent laryngeal nerve.
Now we have isolated the lymph nodal packet.
We have mobilized from all around that is the visceral fascia has been dissected from close to the trachea.
The pre vascular fascia is dissected from the iota and the arch and the recurrent laryngeal nerve is now clearly seen.

within this lymph nodal packet called the left recurrent laryngeal lymph node packet and now the recurrent laryngeal nerve on the left side is dissected from both above and below and isolated till we reach the lateral side that is the prevascular fascia as you can see marked by the black arrow we are going right close to the left recurrent laryngeal nerve we should not use any energy source to cause any kind of injury to the recurrent laryngeal nerve
Small bleeders can be easily controlled at this level by just putting a gauze for pressure points.
This plane is completely avascular and hence it is important to stay as close to the recurrent laryngeal nerve as possible.
Once this is done, we go to the prevascular fascia where we had initially started off and we have joined both from the medial and the lateral side, lifting up all the prevascular
pretracheal and left recurrent laryngeal lymph nodes along with into the specimen.
The whole of the left recurrent laryngeal nerve is completely dissected.
This is the way to perform an easy left recurrent laryngeal lymph node dissection.
